name: Serqet
description: Serqet, also known as Selket, Selqet, or Serkis, is a powerful goddess in Egyptian mythology, primarily associated with protection from venomous creatures, particularly scorpions. Her name means 'she who tightens the throat,' referring both to the constriction of a scorpion's sting and to Serqet's power to heal and protect against it. She is depicted as a woman with a scorpion on her head, or sometimes as a scorpion with a woman's head. Serqet was believed to be a fierce protector of the pharaoh and was often depicted alongside other protective deities such as Isis, Nephthys, and Neith. She played a vital role in funerary rituals, guarding the canopic jar containing the intestines, ensuring the deceased's safe passage into the afterlife. Serqet was also a goddess of healing and magic, invoked to cure scorpion stings and other ailments. Her worship was widespread throughout Egypt, and she was revered for her ability to both inflict and prevent harm. She was considered a skilled magician and a powerful protector, making her an essential figure in Egyptian religious beliefs and practices. Serqet's association with scorpions connects her to both danger and healing, highlighting the duality of her nature and her importance in maintaining balance in the world.
subtitle: Goddess of Protection and Healing

keyMyths.title: Serqet and Horus
keyMyths.description: Serqet protected the infant Horus from scorpion stings while he was hidden from Set. Her magic and protective abilities were crucial in ensuring Horus' survival.

keyMyths.title: The Four Protective Goddesses
keyMyths.description: Serqet, along with Isis, Nephthys, and Neith, guarded the deceased pharaoh in the afterlife. Each goddess protected a specific canopic jar, ensuring the preservation of the organs.

keyMyths.title: Serqet's Healing Power
keyMyths.description: Serqet was invoked to heal scorpion stings and other venomous bites. Her priests and priestesses were skilled in using spells and remedies to counteract the effects of poison.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.author: George Hart
sources.work: A Dictionary of Egyptian Gods and Goddesses
sources.description: Alphabetical listing of Egyptian deities with detailed descriptions.
sources.text: Alphabetical listing of Egyptian deities with detailed descriptions.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, drink, and precious items were made to Serqet in her temples to seek her protection from venomous creatures and ensure healing from stings. Priests and priestesses dedicated to Serqet would perform rituals and prayers on behalf of the worshippers.

worshipPractices.name: Amulets and Charms
worshipPractices.description: Amulets and charms depicting Serqet, often in the form of scorpions or with her image, were worn by individuals to ward off venomous attacks and ensure protection during travel, especially in desert regions. These amulets were believed to hold her protective power.

festivals.name: Serqet's Festival
festivals.description: While specific details are scarce, it's likely Serqet had dedicated festival days involving processions, prayers, and offerings to honor her role as protector against venom and healer of stings. These festivals would have been significant in regions prone to scorpion infestations.

cultural.modernLegacy: Serqet's legacy endures in modern Egyptology and Neopaganism. She is recognized as a symbol of protection, healing, and the power of the natural world. Her image appears in art, literature, and spiritual practices, representing the ability to overcome adversity and find healing in the face of danger.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Serqet's origins are rooted in the early periods of Egyptian history, with evidence of her worship dating back to the Predynastic Period. While specific myths detailing her birth are scarce, she is generally considered a primordial goddess, existing from the earliest times. She is often associated with the sun god Ra, and some accounts place her as his daughter, linking her to divine power and cosmic order. Her emergence as a distinct deity likely stemmed from the very real threat posed by scorpions and other venomous creatures in the Egyptian landscape. As such, she was seen as essential for survival, embodying the protective forces needed to combat these dangers. The earliest depictions of Serqet are found on funerary objects, highlighting her role in protecting the deceased in the afterlife. Over time, her importance grew, leading to the establishment of temples and dedicated priesthoods in her honor.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Serqet doesn't feature prominently in many well-known Egyptian myths, her role is vital in several key narratives. She is one of the four goddesses who protect the canopic jars containing the organs of the deceased, specifically guarding the jar containing the intestines. This association underscores her protective function and her connection to the afterlife. In the myth of Isis and Osiris, Serqet plays a crucial role in protecting the infant Horus from venomous creatures while Isis searches for the scattered pieces of Osiris. She sends scorpions as guards, but a tragic accident leads to the death of a child, prompting Serqet to use her healing powers to revive the child and demonstrate her compassion. These stories highlight Serqet's power to not only inflict venom but also to heal and protect, emphasizing her dual nature as both a dangerous and benevolent deity. Her involvement in these myths solidifies her importance in Egyptian religious beliefs and practices.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Serqet's worship was widespread throughout Egypt, with dedicated temples and shrines established in her honor. Priests and priestesses played a crucial role in performing rituals and offering prayers to seek her protection from venomous creatures and ensure healing from stings. Amulets and charms depicting Serqet, often in the form of scorpions or with her image, were worn by individuals to ward off venomous attacks and ensure protection during travel, especially in desert regions. The most significant center of her cult was located in the Delta region, where scorpions were particularly prevalent. Serqet was also associated with other protective deities, such as Isis and Nephthys, and her image often appeared alongside theirs in funerary contexts. Her role as a protector extended beyond the living, as she was believed to safeguard the deceased in the afterlife, guiding them through the dangers of the underworld. The enduring popularity of Serqet's cult demonstrates the importance of protection and healing in ancient Egyptian society.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Serqet's legacy continues to resonate in modern times through Egyptology and contemporary spiritual practices. Her image and symbolism are frequently used in art, literature, and popular culture to represent protection, healing, and the power of the natural world. In Neopaganism and Wicca, Serqet is often invoked as a guardian deity, offering protection from harm and guidance through difficult times. Her association with scorpions and venom has also led to her being seen as a symbol of transformation and the ability to overcome challenges. The study of Serqet's role in ancient Egyptian religion provides valuable insights into the beliefs and practices of this ancient civilization, shedding light on their understanding of the natural world and their efforts to ensure safety and well-being. Her enduring presence in modern culture demonstrates the timeless appeal of her message of protection and healing, reminding us of the importance of respecting the power of nature and seeking solutions to overcome adversity.

shortDescription: Serqet is an Egyptian goddess of protection from venom and stings, particularly scorpions. She is a guardian deity, often associated with healing and magic.
